sub: How to communicate effectively with AI and manage its limitations

title: Prompt Engineering for Developers
desc: The most transferable daily skill in AI-assisted development.

items: Explicit criteria > vague instructions — “method ≤20 lines, no static” instead of “write clean code”
Few-shot examples — show 2–3 input/output pairs instead of explaining the pattern in words
Structured output — “respond as JSON: {severity, file, line, issue}” for guaranteed parseable format
The interview pattern — “before implementing, ask me 3 clarifying questions”

body.quote: Good prompts are not about being polite or verbose. They are about removing ambiguity.

body.code: // BAD: vague "Check that the comments are accurate." // GOOD: explicit criteria "Flag comments ONLY when the claimed behavior contradicts the actual code behavior. Ignore style preferences and subjective opinions." // BAD: vague "Write good tests." // GOOD: explicit criteria "Write JUnit 5 tests using AAA pattern. Cover: happy path, null input, boundary values. Each test must have exactly one assertion."

body.quote: Specificity is not about length. It is about removing ways for the AI to misinterpret you.

title: Few-Shot Examples: Show, Don’t Tell
desc: Few-shot = giving the model a few worked examples in the prompt. 2–3 examples eliminate ambiguity better than paragraphs of instructions.

body.code: "Review this code. Format findings like these examples: Example 1: File: Calculator.java:15 Severity: ERROR Issue: Division by zero not handled Fix: Add zero-check before divide() Example 2: File: Student.java:42 Severity: WARN Issue: equals() without hashCode() Fix: Override hashCode() using same fields"
body.note: The model generalizes from examples to novel patterns. Show edge cases in your examples.

title: Structured Output: Make Responses Parseable
desc: Ask for a schema, not prose. Output is far more likely to be machine-readable — but still validate before using it.

body.code: // BAD: prose output "Review this file and tell me the issues." → Free-form paragraphs. Fine to read. Impossible to pipe into a script. // GOOD: structured output "Review this file. Respond as JSON: [ { "severity": "ERROR | WARN | INFO", "file": "path/to/file.java", "line": 42, "issue": "short description", "fix": "concrete suggestion" } ]"
body.note: JSON, Markdown tables, CSV — pick a shape a tool can read. A CI script can then parse findings and fail the build on any ERROR-severity row.

items: Downstream automation — scripts consume the output directly; no prose parsing
Batch aggregation — combine findings across many files or PRs into one table
Forces specificity — every row commits to severity / file / line instead of hedging in prose
Comparable runs — same schema across runs lets you diff outputs and spot drift

title: The Interview Pattern
desc: Let the AI ask questions before implementing — like a senior developer would.

body.code: // Instead of: "Implement a caching layer for the grade lookups." // Use the interview pattern: "I need a caching layer for grade lookups. Before implementing, ask me questions about: - Invalidation strategy - Expected load and data volume - Failure modes and fallback behavior - Consistency requirements"
body.note: Claude asks → you think through design → better architecture before any code is written.

body.quote: The interview pattern forces design thinking. You get better results AND better understanding.

title: Context Windows: The Invisible Constraint
desc: Every AI model has a maximum amount of text it can process at once.

body.code: Context window = everything the model can see System prompt + CLAUDE.md rules + conversation history + file contents read + tool outputs + your current request ________________________ = total tokens consumed When you exceed the limit: older context gets compressed or dropped
body.note: Token ≈ a word chunk the model reads (roughly 4 characters of English, or ~0.75 words). Claude Code’s context: 200K tokens standard, up to 1M on Opus/Sonnet extended. Sounds huge — but a large codebase fills it fast.

rows: Extract key findings into a persistent summary pinned at the top
Don’t paste 500 lines of logs — extract the relevant 10
Write findings to a file that survives context compaction
Isolate verbose exploration in a subagent, return only summary
Claude Code slash command — summarizes older turns in-place to free up tokens while keeping essential facts
Claude Code CLI flag — relaunches a prior session so context carries across terminal restarts

body.quote: Context management is not about saving tokens. It is about keeping the AI focused on what matters now.

title: Context Compaction Is Lossy
desc: Long sessions eventually get compacted. Anything you haven’t anchored in durable storage may lose fidelity.

items: Write critical facts to scratchpad files (persist outside context)
Use CLAUDE.md for facts that must never be lost
Restate key constraints in your current message (recent turns are preserved verbatim)

body.quote: Don’t trust the compactor with anything you can’t re-derive. Pin it down yourself.

title: Human-in-the-Loop: When to Escalate
desc: Not every decision should be automated. Define escalation triggers explicitly — don’t rely on the model to self-assess.

rows: Routine, reversible (read files, run tests, run linter)
AI presents options with trade-offs; human chooses
table.note: Triggers are things you define and the model can observe. Self-assessed confidence is not a reliable trigger — LLMs are miscalibrated.

body.card: Prompt AI to report these — then verify:
items: State assumptions — “I assumed X was Y; flag if wrong” > silent guessing
Report partial results — what succeeded, what failed, what was skipped
Name coverage gaps — “I reviewed 8 of 12 files, 4 were too large” > “done”
Name the failure mode — a bare “failed” is useless; you cannot decide next steps without why

title: CLAUDE.md: Standing Rules for AI Coding
desc: Pin these at the top of CLAUDE.md. They survive context compression and become the AI’s defaults.

body.code: # Coding rules - Ask questions before implementing. If multiple interpretations exist, present them — do not pick silently. - State success criteria before writing code. Make them verifiable (tests, checks, examples). - Prefer the simplest solution that meets the requirement. No speculative abstractions. - Touch only what the request needs. No drive-by refactors. Match existing style.
body.note: These four rules recap techniques from this course: Interview Pattern and Explicit Criteria (this module), KISS & YAGNI (Module 8, Parsimony Principle). The fourth — surgical changes — is the one you have not seen yet, and it is the single most common LLM coding failure in practice.

body.quote: CLAUDE.md is the AI’s standing orders. Everything else is a one-off instruction that gets forgotten after /compact.

title: Exercise 7.1: Rewrite a Vague Prompt
desc: 20 minutes — apply all four techniques to a real task.

items: “Review my Calculator class and make it better.”

body.card: Rewrite it using all four techniques:
items: 1. Explicit criteria — what counts as “better”? List 3–5 concrete rules
2. Few-shot — give 2 examples of acceptable review findings
3. Structured output — specify the exact JSON/table format for the response
4. Interview pattern — ask Claude to clarify 3 things before reviewing

body.card: Run both prompts on the same code. Compare:
items: Which response is easier to act on?
Which response is easier to parse with a script?
Did the rewritten prompt catch issues the vague one missed?
How much longer is the rewritten prompt — and was it worth it?

body.quote: The goal is not a perfect prompt. The goal is to feel the difference between vague and specific — so you notice it in your own daily prompts.

title: Exercise 7.2: Watch Compaction Happen
desc: 15 minutes — observe what compaction preserves and what it drops.

items: Pick a task with specific details — a bug with a stack trace, a config with concrete values, or a file with named fields and line numbers
Have a conversation with Claude Code about it — include specific numbers, file paths, error strings, and at least one decision you change your mind about mid-conversation
Run /compact
Ask Claude to recall 3 specific facts from before the compaction: “What was the error code? Which file was at line 42? What did I decide about the timeout value?”
Note which survived verbatim, which got paraphrased, which disappeared
Which types of facts survived — numbers, names, decisions, reasoning chains?
Which got softened or generalized beyond usefulness?
Which facts would you have pinned in CLAUDE.md or a scratchpad ahead of compaction?

body.quote: You cannot predict what compaction will drop until you see it happen. Do this once — it changes how you structure long sessions.

rows: Prompt quality is the most transferable daily skill in AI-assisted development
The single most powerful prompting technique
JSON / tables make responses parseable — the bridge to CI
Let AI ask clarifying questions before implementing
Finite — manage them or lose critical information
Anchor facts that must survive context compaction
Irreversible actions always require human approval, regardless of AI confidence
